detail: expected rail 'LPC_VCC' neither appears in source_quote nor maps to any registry signal alias cited in the quote

original_draft.source_quote: Board completely unresponsive after being left plugged-in while off — no LCD, no LPC wakeup indication, nothing happens when the power key is pressed. Likely cause: LPC system controller enters an unrecoverable state; a firmware bug prevents it from leaving its locked-up condition.

evidence.reasoning: The dump names 'LPC system controller' (alias of 'LPC controller'). The functional bridge maps 'LPC controller' to refdes candidate U14 (sources rail LPC_VCC).
Board described as completely unresponsive with no LPC wakeup indication — modelled as LPC controller U14 dead.

evidence.source_quote_substring: no LCD, no LPC wakeup indication
evidence.reasoning: With LPC dead, LPC_VCC (sourced by U14 per graph) loses its load/source role and downstream LPC-gated functionality goes dark.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: LPC firmware lockup leaves the LPC controller (U14) unresponsive; the board can't be woken. Simulated as U14 dead; its LPC_VCC rail is the directly affected rail.

original_draft.source_quote: It stopped working: it was not turning on, and when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark.

evidence.reasoning: Source names 'D9 LED' directly; bridge maps to refdes D9 (exact token match).
LED reported as dark with supply present — indicator LED itself open / failed is one of the named candidate causes.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: Low-probability but legitimate candidate per rule-dead-d9-dark-004: indicator LED D9 itself failed open. Silent local failure — no cascading rails die, just the indicator.

original_draft.source_quote: It stopped working: it was not turning on, and when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark. Likely cause: LPC firmware or power-path fault preventing the system from initialising; often triggered after deep-discharge or battery mishandling events.

evidence.reasoning: Dump attributes the failure to 'LPC firmware' — 'LPC' is an alias of 'LPC controller'; bridge maps to U14.
Whole board fails to turn on; modelled as LPC controller dead.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: After deep-discharge event, LPC firmware is corrupted and LPC does not initialise; board stays off and D9 dark. Modelled as U14 dead, taking LPC_VCC down.

original_draft.source_quote: One USB port was not functioning properly; subsequently the kernel log filled at high speed with `reform2_lpc spi1.0: lpc_command: c/0 checksum mismatch: af expected, 0 received`. LPC SPI communication broken — often correlated with a concurrent USB port malfunction, possibly suggesting a power or ground integrity problem on the SPI bus.

evidence.reasoning: 'reform2_lpc' is a listed alias of 'LPC controller'; bridge maps to U14.
LPC no longer responds correctly on SPI; checksum mismatches imply LPC not functional — modelled as dead.
Graph rail 'spi1.0' is aliased to LPC_SPI_BUS; when U14 is dead the LPC SPI bus loses its slave endpoint.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: Broken LPC SPI communication with checksum flood modelled as U14 dead, taking down the LPC SPI bus (spi1.0 alias present literally in the source quote).

original_draft.source_quote: A prior report was incorrectly attributed to a detached port but turned out to be a faulty cable; a later confirmed case involved the port actually detaching after upgrading to a newer motherboard + sound board.

evidence.reasoning: Mechanical detachment modelled as connector open.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: Intentionally low-confidence: the micro-HDMI port has no refdes candidate in the bridge, so this scenario cannot be properly attributed. Kept only to signal the failure class; will likely be discarded downstream as the cause.refdes cannot be validly grounded. Skipping further micro-HDMI scenarios per the bridge (no candidate).

original_draft.source_quote: When the charge board is dead, other community posts describe connecting pins 2 and 3 on the J1 plug to bypass it temporarily and allow firmware reflashing while a replacement board is sourced.

evidence.reasoning: 'J1 plug' is a listed alias of 'J1 connector'; bridge maps literally to refdes J1.
Loss of battery/charge board connectivity via J1 is equivalent to J1 path open from the motherboard's perspective.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: J1 is the sole motherboard connector to the battery/charge board. If J1 opens (equivalently, the charge board is dead and the cable yields no continuity), the per-cell BAT1..BAT8 rails from the charge board become unavailable on the motherboard.
